a.headline: AI Michael Caine narrates Odyssey audiobook
a.summary: ElevenLabs has launched an officially licensed AI replica of Michael Caine's voice to narrate a retelling of Homer's Odyssey — a sign that synthetic voice performance is now entering mainstream publishing and media production. As a designer or motion practitioner, this raises urgent questions about the future of creative labour in audio-visual storytelling and where the line between licensed likeness and authentic performance sits.

a.headline: TwelveLabs Brings Its Video Understanding Technology Directly to Creators
a.summary: TwelveLabs has released Rodeo, an application-layer product that brings AI video understanding tools directly into creators' hands — a significant step toward AI-assisted editing and synthesis becoming standard in motion design workflows. If you're working in video or motion, this is the kind of tool worth monitoring closely as it signals where production pipelines are heading.

a.headline: Readers say 'there's no such thing as an AI artist' in this week's Dezeen Debate
a.summary: Dezeen's reader debate around AI-generated renders of Gaudí's speculative New York hotel has sparked a pointed public argument about whether 'AI artist' is a legitimate professional identity. For design students, this debate is directly relevant — it maps the contested territory you'll be entering, where authorship, tool use, and creative credit are all up for negotiation.

b.headline: Majority of datacenters are vulnerable to climate threats like floods and fires, study finds
b.summary: A new study warns that AI data centres — the infrastructure powering the generative tools now embedded in design workflows — are critically exposed to the very climate hazards their own emissions are accelerating. For designers using AI image and video tools at scale, this is a stark reminder that every render and generation request has a physical footprint with real environmental consequences.

b.headline: America's data center backlash is bipartisan — can it stay that way?
b.summary: Growing community opposition to AI data centre construction is now crossing political lines in the US, with residents and local governments pushing back against the land, water, and energy demands of AI infrastructure. As creatives who are increasingly dependent on cloud-based generative tools, it's worth understanding that the backlash against this infrastructure is intensifying — and that political pressure could reshape how and where these tools are hosted.

c.headline: Hundreds of Artists Ask NYC Mayor to Ban AI in Schools
c.summary: Artists including Nan Goldin and Molly Crabapple have signed an open letter urging New York City's mayor to ban AI tools from schools, framing AI adoption as a direct threat to the city's creative future and the development of genuine artistic skill. This is a significant act of collective opposition — and a direct challenge to the narrative that AI in education is neutral or inevitable.

c.headline: Sick of being ripped off? Raymond Biesinger illustrates a self-defense guide for creatives
c.summary: Illustrator Raymond Biesinger has channelled years of being underpaid and exploited into a book that equips creatives to protect themselves from the industry's worst actors. In a climate where AI is devaluing creative labour and clients are increasingly emboldened to cut corners, this kind of self-advocacy resource feels more necessary than ever.

c.headline: Maybe What We Need Right Now Is More Art Nouveau
c.summary: This piece argues that TCA Ethera and Art Nouveau's celebration of ornament, nature, and individuality feels like a deliberate counter-position to the flattened, algorithmically averaged aesthetics of the AI era. It's a useful provocation for design students thinking about how historical movements can inform a critical response to digital sameness.
